comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's just shy of midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Michael Sendivogius -- that's Michał Sędziwój if you happen to be fluent in Polish -- one of the great alchemists of the later European tradition. Born in 1566, he studied with several important alchemists of the period, and finally learned the secret of transmutation from the Scots alchemist Alexander Seton after helping Seton escape from the dungeons of the ruler of Saxony. Like most alchemists of his time, Sendivogius traveled from country to country, seeking patronage from monarchs and aristocrats who were willing to help fund his experiments. He was among other things the first person to sketch out the nitrogen cycle in nature and to recognize the role of oxygen in supporting life. He died in 1636. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This week's Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

comments.title: Atheist to Christian
comments.content:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a0xCs2EfiXA

This interview with British historian Tom Holland is a great data point or bellwether for anyone interested in our host's prediction that a portion of the early 21st century's atheist movement will eventually turn to Christianity.(and neopagans - he says at the start that since he was a boy he liked the Greek gods.) It is video but can be listened to as audio, all the important content is spoken.

It is also a great account of an intelligent person explaining a journey on which he changed his mind - during the writing of his new book Dominion. (Subtitled in the US "How the Christian Revolution Remade the World" and in the UK "The Making of the Western Mind".)

He is talking from the UK perspective on Christianity where the liberal Church of England is the main face of public Christianity and atheism is the norm among educated people, so it is quite different from the US.

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content: I'd be interested in seeing a transcript if there is one. As you describe it, though, it's exactly the sort of thing I'd expect to see -- what makes the Second Religiosity pick up is an interesting combination of working class believers who've never abandoned their faith and intellectuals who've run into the hard limits of reason.

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content:
Morality rather than reason was what changed him. He was finding how much of liberal secular morality sprung from Christianity rather than from the Classical world, and he found it revelatory what a change it was in human history and thought for compassion and respect for the weak to become important values. One example that particularly affected him was the exposure of babies by the Greeks and Romans. He talks about early saints who were involved in rescuing them.

It wouldn't be surprising if interviews with similar content are eventually published in print. But the book is only just out in the US, where the people are more likely to be interested in someone turning to Christianity. The video interview is with an Australian preacher, not a British interviewer.

This is a book for the sort of audience who read authors like Stephen Pinker and other big-idea nonfiction so it has the potential to be influential. Though the US cover is overtly Christian.

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content:
One of the limits of reason is that it's very hard to base morality on it. Arguing from "is" to "ought," from facts to values, is almost always fallacious -- and yet that's very nearly the only resource the atheist has. That limitation of reason, in turn, is one of the things that helps drive the rise of the Second Religiosity in the history of every civilization.

Holland's quite correct, though, that modern secular morality is secular only on the surface; scratch the paint and it's Christian through and through -- the good parts and also the bad ones. The same Christian morality that brought compassion and respect for the weak also brought violent intolerance concerning differences of opinion, which of course we also see plenty of from atheists and other would-be secular thinkers!

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content:
The entire notion of "human rights" in and of itself is arguably predicated on Christian morality - the idea being that since the rights are granted by God, they are God's to take away and not, say, a king's.

Take the first article of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights: "All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood."

Sentence 1 is an affirmation. Try to prove it scientifically - you can't. Where's the "dignity gene" and the "rights gene"? How would you even set up a controlled experiment for that? Which means it's a values statement operating on certain assumptions.

Sentence 2, which actually contains "should," is more explicit that it's a values judgment. The "endowed with reason and conscience" part is the only one that might be a scientifically provable statement.

Nothing about the Enlightenment was inevitable. You could very easily have had slavery in the industrial world: just move the slaves from fields to factories. (In fact, we arguably do; it's just that the slaves are in other countries so we don't think about it that much.)

- Barrigan

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content: The current idea of inherent, innate human rights is indeed Christian in origin. The classical world had instead the concept of the rights of the citizen -- and much of the political history of the Western world since the Renaissance can be seen as the collision between those two very distinct visions of what rights are or should be.

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content: Oswald Spengler made a similar point in The Decline of the West. He noted that notions of "universal human rights" or "universal human values" aren't universal at all (other high cultures such as the Classical and Mesoamerican Cultures have had very different sets of values), but in reality are based on the values of the liberal elites, which in turn derive from Christian moral teachings.

replies.title: Re: Atheist to Christian
replies.content:
Indeed he did. Spengler's work has had a major influence on my thinking -- he helped me recognize just how much of what gets treated as universally true is simply a set of habits inherent to the culture in which I was raised.

That doesn't make those habits bad, btw -- just not universally true.

comments.title: Problems with starting SOP
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
after several months of indecision and half-hearted attempts, I finally began seriously doing the SOP about six or seven weeks ago. Progress was slow (I’m up to the calling of Fire), in part because of a whole series of little negative events in my life: a small but very nasty domestic accident, a filthy, flu-like episode still in progress, the return of chronic insomnia, personal disputes, and the theft of some valuable things while I was in a shop. All of which has drained energy and time that might have been devoted to better things. I don’t feel very protected somehow. So far as I can see I am doing the rituals correctly, and finding them more familiar and pleasing, although I have been too tired recently to devote as much time as they probably need.
So what’s going on? My divinatory tool all my life has been the I Ching, and I asked it what was going on. The first hexagram was 41 (Decrease) with moving lines indicating sacrifice of things and possibly (depending on the translation), too much time and effort devoted to others, and the second hexagram was 40 (Deliverance), which is optimistic on the face of it for the longer term.
Any idea what’s this is all about, and any suggestions on what I should do?
Thanks you.

replies.title: Re: Problems with starting SOP
replies.content:
Okay, what's going on is common enough that I probably need to add something about it to the Magic Monday FAQ. Trad occult literature calls it "karmic culmination." When you begin serious spiritual practice, various bits of unresolved business come to the surface in a hurry, and you end up getting a flurry of bad luck for a while. It's temporary, and once it passes off you'll find that a bunch of old issues have gone away for good, but yes, it can be very annoying while it's happening.

That is to say, the Book of Changes was as usual spot on. You're Decreasing your old karma and will be Delivered from it as you continue to practice.

replies.title: Re: Problems with starting SOP
replies.content: The interesting thing is that since I began practices like divination, meditation and the Sphere of Protection, there was not much of karmic culmination. In began these practices more or less in spring 2019.

replies.title: Re: Problems with starting SOP
replies.content: I've been doing the SOP for about two or three months now, and I haven't really noticed any bad luck recently. Am I doing the SOP wrong, is the karmic culmination not yet here, or did I just not have a whole lot to begin with?

replies.title: Re: Problems with starting SOP
replies.content: You may just not have a lot of karmic trouble to clear away. It really does vary.

Dear JMG,

I'm up to the point in the DMH where I've started scrying the elements during the grove ceremony. The threefold practice of the SOP, meditation, and Ogham divination has been enormously helpful. I do all three every morning, and they have become an indispensable daily habit. I wish I had started with the DMH earlier in life.

Yet I seem to be pushed further into my job; I have a good reputation at work and I keep being trusted with more complex things. Still, my gut feeling tells me that in the absence of a clear opportunity in the medium term to escape the rat race, I should stay put, learn the lessons it has for me and get my house in order as per the Path of the Hearth-Fire. I did some meditation and an Ogham reading on the question of what I need to know if I stay in my job and accept the promotion: Reversed Beith, Reversed Fearn, and Luis, which shows that I although I think my job has been a waste of time, that if I avoid being wilfully blind to the opportunity the job presents and pay attention that I will often be out of my depth, I will learn from it and more choices will be available down the road. comments.title: Ogham casting
comments.content:
Hello,
I would like to ask about Ogham casting. I have been practising it for several months now and I have developed some personal understanding of it, but I would like to make sure I am not on the wrong track.

It feels to me like a kind of communication using a common language that exists in the form of Ogham fews but can be developed in a personal way between me and those (whoever they are) that answer. Can it be like between people who have known each other for a long time that certain words have special tinge comprehensible just to the people involved or even that they are used in a different way entirely?

There are two reasons why I am asking about it:
1. I have so far encoutered several sources on the meaning of Ogham fews and they differ in the meanings, they differ in the final aicme and they differ in the trees attributed to the fews in the first aicme. Still, all these systems work for the people who use them. I simply chose one and stick to it, but is this diversity possible because the other side knows which system I am using and what the fews mean to me?

2. I use visualisation of the fews that is presented in Paul Rhys Mountfort’s book. Doing so I add details to the scene that he does not describe. Yet during one of the previous castings I asked a question and the first few laid down and the image I had in my head so precisly corresponded to the question asked that I was stunned.

Am I imagining things too much and inventing something that is not happening or could it possibly work the way I described?

Thank you,

Hana

replies.title: Re: Ogham casting
replies.content: That's exactly how it works. The other side of the divinatory process works with what's in your head, not with what's in any given book -- that's why people who have completely different interpretations of the symbols of an oracle can both get accurate divinations with it. When you add details to a scene in your imagination, you're enriching the language of imagery in ways that give the intelligences of the Ogham more to work with, so it's no wonder you're getting good results.

replies.title: Re: Ogham casting
replies.content: What are the traces which hints to an earlier order of the first aicme which was BLNFS instead of the order BLFSN, which is the order which was used on the historic Ogham inscriptions?

replies.title: Re: Ogham casting
replies.content: On the one hand, the Ogham alphabet is always called "Beith-Luis-Nion" -- not "Beith-Luis-Fearn." On the other, BLNVS (F is pronounced V) spells the name of the Pagan Celtic god Belenos...which explains why the order was changed.

comments.title: Cosmic Tides / Invisible Helpers
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. As I re-read “The Magical Battle of Britain”, I have been struck by several references by Dion Fortune to “Cosmic tides” which she does not explain, likely because all her students are already familiar with the term. I, however, am not familiar with it and cannot tell from the timing of the references as to whether they are particular combinations of solar and lunar phenomena (e.g., waxing Moon during the “brightening half” of the year) or something more subtle. Can you shed any light on it?

A second question. During the bombing of London (September 1940), Fortune wrote, “… the stress of an air raid heightens psychic perception, and many a person will witness the parting of the Veil and see into the Unseen who, under normal conditions, might have to work long before they achieved such an experience”. This almost sounds like a psychological matter, where the additional stress causes a piercing of the veil… When I read this, I reflected on the Hindu concept of the yugas, wherein our present age (Kali) has the “curse” of life being more brutal and short, but the “blessing” that any spiritual effort made during this age has many times the efficacy of spiritual efforts made in the more “spiritual” ages. So, in this way, being in the midst of a bomb raid is kind-of like “Kali yuga on steroids”! But there is more to it than sheer intensity of experience, since a couple of paragraphs later, Fortune states, “[i]f the turmoil can be stilled even for a few seconds, it will be possible to pick up the inner contacts with every probability of vision opening and enabling us to see the work going on the Inner Planes, for the Invisible Helpers come very close to earth at these times” (p.58). So, it would appear that it is not entirely psychological. Perhaps these “Invisible Helpers” are more accessible during times when so many souls are passing through the Veil (i.e., people dying en masse). Or perhaps there is another reason for their close proximity to earth during such times. Any further information on this matter would be most appreciated!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Tides / Invisible Helpers
replies.content:
1) The Cosmic Tides are indeed solar and lunar. The Sun governs the astral tides, the Moon the etheric tides. In both cases, the brightening half is divided into two parts, a tide of cleansing in the first half and a tide of sowing in the second; the darkening half is likewise divided into a tide of reaping in the first half and a tide of storing, planning, and preparation in the second.

2) It's pretty much all of the above. The threat of sudden death makes it hard to stay comfortably settled in one's habitual mental ruts -- and when many people are dying, yes, there are helping spirits who come close to the world of manifestation to help them through.

comments.title: The Incident
comments.content:
I lead a group at an office where people supplied by several smaller businesses service a much larger concern. The work is done under difficult circumstances and under a good deal of pressure but in our area most of the people involved are surprisingly well balanced. I'm not aware of much overt office politics and everyone seems friendly.

About this time last week there was a small accident that had a temporary impact on our customers. It was handled efficiently, and scarcely lasted more than 15 minutes before everything was restored to normal. Things like this have been pretty rare while I've been around although they happened regularly in the past, and even these days they are generally looked on as learning opportunities and life moves on.

On this occasion however there were some complaints about how our communications to the rest of the office had been handled and groups supplied by other businesses demanded a full scale meeting to discuss "lessons that could be learned". Concern trolling of this kind is unprecedented.

I did all the things that I would have done normally to deal with this in the past, and then, gathering the few scraps of esoteric knowledge that I've managed to pick up here in the last few years stalled the meeting until after Mercury retrograde.

A geomantic divination asking whether my job was under attack ended up with Cauda Draconis in the 1st house, Carcer in the tenth house perfected by conjunction with Carcer in the 12th. Left and right witnesses were both Fortuana Minor and the Judge was Populus.

From this I concluded that there were many forces at work but this was a deliberate attack using the accident as a pretext. The attack would fail with some outside help and that I would not have to do too much to make this happen but that this occasion did represent an ending of part of my professional life. There was a much much more overt attempt to take over the work of my team a few months ago so this seemed to a reasonable interpretation.

I made a heartfelt prayer to the father of my pantheon, stacked the meeting with some high powered personalities and despite some grumbling from some of the attendees the whole thing came off pretty much as I wanted with no further consequences to my team members. I've decided to reduce my already minimal involvement in the interesting day to day detail of our work to concentrate more on the performance of the systems we deal with and more interaction with other teams and people at a managerial level. Not my strong suit but I'll do my best and I expect I can learn what's needed.

So first of all - TSW, obviously. Thanks to what I have managed to learn here I was able to head off something resembling an attempt at an ambush although there are going to be some personal consequences. Completely losing this work at this juncture in my life would have had consequences for many more than just myself and I'm grateful for the teaching here that's allowed me to cope with it. I would have simply walked unknowingly into the situation at some other stages in my life.

Secondly, I'm certainly not seeking revenge or to take advantage of any little problems that the originating parties are having. I'm familiar with the raspberry jam effect; but there's an inevitable but.

Is there anything I can do to discourage this sort of thing in the future? I'm trying to approach this with the same sort of attitude I've got to keeping raccoons out of my bins and the local cats using my front drive as a litter box. They are what they are and I don't want to harm them. I'm happy to make them think twice though.

replies.title: Re: The Incident
replies.content: Glad to hear that you got through it fine. In your place I'd rely on prayer and divination as a source of guidance and protection.

comments.title: What have I committed to?
comments.content:
JMG:
More years ago than I like to contemplate, I joined AODA and self-initiated as a candidate. In the years since, I’ve done all of the practices in the first-year curriculum at some time, to some level- but never all at the same time, thoroughly, in the same year. I’m finding that my family demands and especially my utter lack of privacy from curious young eyes mean that it will be some years before I have the time and focus to “do it right”. Some practices, like the SoP and my Earth path habits, have stuck, while others, like regular meditation, I’ve repeatedly started and fallen away from. (My kids always, always wake up and come investigate – even when I’ve tried starting at 4:30 AM. I think they sense it, like cats!)

I take seriously your advice to finish what you start, and indeed, am trying to address the same habit of taking on too much without assessing my situation to see if it will fit, in many other areas of my life as well. I’ve accepted that I made a commitment to work on these practices simultaneously for the period of a year, and I’m imitating the Little Way of fitting in whatever I can, whenever I can, until the time that my family commitments (and, I will acknowledge, my own self-discipline) allow me to do that. I see the practices as beneficial and believe I will grow from incorporating them properly into my life, but I find that I’m no longer interested in progressing through the AODA’s higher degrees once these commitments are completed. I don’t feel connected to that organization.

My question is this: what, exactly, have I committed to on a spiritual level? Once initiated as a Candidate, am I “set on the path” of progressing through AODA’s ranks, or is it OK to take the tools I learn as a Candidate in different directions? Is there some closure and leaving ceremony I should carry out once I complete my Candidate tasks? (Incidentally, when I initiated myself, I envisioned Sara in her role as Archdruid of Water as leading me through the ceremony, with no idea at all of the implications. My apologies to her if I have entangled her in my unfinished business.) These questions are spurred by your comment some time back that once you start on the path of (was it spiritual development? Or magical practice? I can’t find it now…), the door is opened and there is no real turning back. That thought shook me; I realized that if I’m to take seriously the idea that TSW, then of course my actions of starting on a path have consequences. I’d appreciate your advice on how far those consequences extend.

Thanks-
--Heather in CA

replies.title: Re: What have I committed to?
replies.content:
Not the OP, but a related query. I formally left the AODA (by my own free choice) some time ago, and at that time, set aside the Sphere of Protection practice and adopted another daily spiritual and magical routine.

If I were to resume the SOP, outside the context of any other connection with the AODA, would I thereby be somehow "reconnecting" myself to that organization and/or its leadership? Or is there some way that I could pick the SOP practice back up while still keeping my distance from the AODA and its leadership?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: What have I committed to?
replies.content: The SoP isn't original to AODA; it belonged to the whole family of magical orders and spiritual groups to which AODA belonged, and there are plenty of people practicing it who have no connection with AODA at all. That's why I've been teaching it more generally to people interested in magical spirituality. You don't have to worry about being linked to AODA in any way.

replies.title: Re: What have I committed to?
replies.content:
You haven't. AODA drew very heavily in its development on the Universal Gnostic tradition, which places the free choice of the individual at the center of its spiritual path. You have started on the path of inner spirituality, but you're not bound to that organization, or to any organization at all. Your awareness of spiritual realities will ripen at its own pace, and you'll likely find life easier in that context if you put some time into some form of spiritual practice, but which direction you take the path is up to you.

When you reach the end of your Candidate work, do a grove ceremony, thank the guardian spirits of the order for their help, tell them that you're going in a different direction, and close in the usual way. That should do the job.

comments.title: Four treasures of Tuatha De Danann
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

There is a strangely well-fitting correspondence between four treasures of Tuatha De Danann and the four elements in ancient Greek mysteries. Is this due to a possible contact between ancient Druids and Greek philosophers, or is it one of those retrospective reconstructions by modern occultists?

replies.title: Re: Four treasures of Tuatha De Danann
replies.content: You might want to see if you can find a copy of Nora Chadwick's excellent book The Druids, which makes a very strong case that ancient Druidry was the exact equivalent in Celtic cultures of that movement we call "presocratic philosophy" among the ancient Greeks. She points in particular to the Pythagorean movement as a very close Greek equivalent of what the old Druids were up to. Her viewpoint was hopelessly unfashionable when it was published and even more so since then -- you get all these unhelpful fantasies about Celts as "noble savages" and of course the Western world's endless fawning misrepresentations about the Greeks -- but I think she was spot on, and that the four treasures are in fact what Irish poetic mythologists made of the four elements.

comments.title: Gods yet to come
comments.content: I was asking for guidance for a deity to talk to and I received the name Maitreya which is supposed to be the 10th avatar (or 11th depending on who you talk to) in the Hindu tradition. I have prayed the usual way and without much success but I’m still working on it. replies.title: Re: Gods yet to come
replies.content: Maitreya already exists -- he just hasn't made his appearance on Earth. In Buddhist teaching, which also reveres him, Maitreya is the senior bodhisattva, gearing up for his incarnation on Earth as the next Buddha, when he will preach the dharma like all the countless Buddhas before and after him. Some Buddhist mystics are believed to have entered into a kind of suspended animation to wait for his appearance.

replies.title: Re: Gods yet to come
replies.content: The root word for Maitreya comes from Sanskrit 'maitr' - "friend". In both Buddhist and Hindu scriptures, sutras, etc it's said he will be The World Teacher to all humanity and will help evolve human consciousness to higher spiritual planes. In Buddhist cosmology specifically it's said Buddha-to-Be Maitreya won't appear for another 5.6 billion years. Furthermore he will appear only long after Buddha Shakyamuni's teachings have all disappeared for many millennia.

replies.title: Re: Gods yet to come
replies.content: That's why Kobo Daishi, for example, is waiting for him -- it was a common belief in medieval Japan that they were already living in the latter days of the dharma, and so the idea was that if you had the spiritual chops, you could wait until the next Buddha showed up and get the dharma in its purest form. Of course Kobo Daishi also dedicated all the merit gained by the next 5.6 billion years of meditation to all sentient beings, but that's what you do when you're Japan's answer to Merlin.

replies.title: Re: Filí
replies.content: The old filidecht went out of existence under violent English repression centuries ago; the hedge schools of the 18th century were their last fading echo. In ancient times the Irish Druids (like Druids everywhere) included the roles of bard (fili) and ovate (faith) as well as druid; after Ireland was converted to Christianity, filidecht was spun off as an independent institution and survived in that form until the English destroyed the economic and cultural basis for their survival.

comments.title: Magic in dance?
comments.content:
Greetings JMG and community. I'm hoping to find some leads on magic in dance.

A little background about myself,I've followed your writings since the Archdruid Report days. They have been a primary influence on my life... leading me to make choices that I hope will allow me to prosper and flourish in the long descent.

Two years ago I started magical training using your Druid Magic Handbook as a guide. At the time, the intensity of it was too much for me, and I had to take a break from it. Recently, however, I have picked it back up again... with daily CoP, meditation, and divination. I'm taking it slower this time around and things feel much more balanced (tracks in space 'n all?).

In between these two periods, I opened a dance studio and have begun teaching dance. As a dance instructor, I get the chance to witness what is being explored, developed, and taught in different dance communities around Europe. With the benefit of the little magical training I have, I have noticed parallels between what is being explored in the dance community, and in magic... especially regarding visualizations and breathing techniques. It is often that dance event weekends bring about profound emotional experiences in the dancers who attend. As an example, there are dance weekends that use tantra techniques to discover dancers' “shadows,” and how they relate to consent relationships during partnered dances.

From my earlier attempt at magical training, I understand how impactful magic can be on one's life, and also a glimmer of the risks that come with dabbling in it. Today in the dance communities I'm involved with, there is a lot of incorporating Eastern traditions into Western dance forms. I wish to better understand what affects this might have on dancers and communities.

As such, I wanted to see if there were any books or resources on the topic of magic in dancing, or dance magic?

Of course, I'll be observing and meditating on my experiences going forward, but any extra insight would be greatly appreciated :) Again, many thanks for all that you do.

Greg, Poland

replies.title: Re: Magic in dance?
replies.content:
Hmm! The only thing I know that relates to this even slightly is the work of Francois Delsarte, who worked out a system of gesture and movement that had close connections to some of the occult schools active in France in his time. Among the practitioners of his system was Genevieve Stebbins, who was a dancer and actress, later a teacher of exercise, and was also a high-ranking adept in the Hermetic Brotherhood of Light.

There was much more back in the day; students of the ancient mysteries in Greek times incorporated dance into their practices. Unfortunately Christianity, with its hostility to the body, banned all that (along with nearly all Greek and Roman music -- it's appalling to realize how little of it survived). So you're venturing into largely unexplored territory; please keep detailed notes and publish what you discover!

replies.title: Re: Magic in dance?
replies.content:
Great! Will do!

Looks like I have a book to write at some point in my life...

Greg

replies.title: Re: Magic in dance?
replies.content:
This is Deborah Bender, who neglected to log in.

About forty years ago, I took a lesson in Isadora Duncan dance from a dancer who had been trained in that dance tradition and who was also Pagan. (It was a pan-pagan gathering, and the other thing she did on the program was an attempt at reconstructing Minoan dance, on the basis of Minoan artwork depicting dancers and IIRC the training she had received in belly dance.) Besides teaching some movements (Duncan's dance style was a precursor to modern dance and the movements are not very complicated), she also instructed us in directing our attention. The only detail I remember from this aspect of the lesson was something like radiating energy from the heart. This came very naturally to me.

Duncan originated her style of dancing which appears to be an attempt to reconstruct the dance of women in ancient Greece, based on their depiction in ancient art. Most of her students were girls and women. It was direct teaching. According to a complaint I read from a latter day male Isadora Duncan dancer and teacher, male participants became unwelcome in the movement after the rise of Second Wave feminism. Knowing my own attitudes at a certain point in my development as a feminist. I think this complaint is credible.

Like many other sorts of dance, this is not something you can learn just from a book or from watching performances. Given how many generations have passed since Isadora Duncan died, I don't know whether the kind of instruction I received was common at the time, whether it continues to be passed down, whether it's passed down by some lineages of teachers but not others, and whether you would be able to find a teacher at all.

However, it is one of the few dance forms in the Western tradition that had overtly spiritual elements at its conception, so it would be worth investigating.

comments.title: Geomantic Chart Gone Wrong
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I cast a chart last week about a then-upcoming test to determine whether or not I had high pressure in my eyes and therefore was at risk of glaucoma. My question was "Will my test today show that I have glaucoma?"

I received the following house chart:

1: Puer
2: Acquisitio
3: Albus
4: Tristitia
5: Laetitia
6: Fortuna Minor
7: Albus
8: Puer
9: Laetitia
10: Fortuna Major
11: Rubeus
12: Via

I read this chart as a yes by placing myself as the querent in the first house and the result of the test in the sixth house, with Fortuna Minor being positive. The test, however, showed I did not have glaucoma, just thick corneas, and so the concerning pressure readings were misleading. I should note (though it may be obvious) that that was the outcome I hoped for, but I specifically asked if the test would show I have glaucoma, not if the test would come out as I hoped.

Is there any way that I am reading this wrong, or was the response simply inaccurate? Am I using the wrong houses, or is there a reason this should be adjusted to third person, such as adjusting the querent to the sixth house for the physician?

A second and related question: how often are castings wrong? I realize you aren't going to give me a percentage or something, but do you find that you make castings sometimes that are just plain wrong (for those with clear yes/no answers you can later check, anyway)? Presumably there are times while casting a chart that the caster can just fail to get properly tuned into the anima mundi?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Chart Gone Wrong
replies.content:
Not JMG:

My thoughts on the matter: There is no mode of perfection between the 1st and 6th house, so the answer to the question asked would be no. The Fortuna Minor in the 6th house is suggestive of "for a good reason."

- Barrigan

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Chart Gone Wrong
replies.content: The chart didn't go wrong, you just misread it. When you ask a question with a house chart, if there isn't a mode of perfection connecting the figures of querent and quesited, the answer to your question is no. It doesn't matter what else is going on in the chart! No mode of perfection connects Puer and Fortuna Minor, so the answer to your question -- "will the test show that I have glaucoma?" -- can only be "no."

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Chart Gone Wrong
replies.content:
Aha. Well, that's helpful to know! I suppose I got myself mixed up when reading "When you've chosen the house of the quesited, see what figure appears there. That figure gives you the most basic answer to your question; a favorable figure is a positive answer, an unfavorable figure is a negative one." So while I noted the lack of a mode of perfection, I was confused in thinking that the favorable figure in the house of the quesited still meant yes.

Given that's not the case, if the figure in the house of the quesited for a yes/no answer contradicts the overall answer of the chart, should I just read that as detail rather than an answer? So in this case, would I just read Fortuna Minor as indicative of the fact that I would be pleased by the results, or what Barrigan above is suggesting that it is for good reason?

Apparently I have more geomantic practice and study ahead of me! (Not that I didn't know that, though such a simple mistake seems an extra confirmation.) :)

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Chart Gone Wrong
replies.content: I'd take Fortuna Minor in the 6th as "you've got some kind of temporary condition which will go away soon, so don't worry."

It frankly never seemed to "click" on a daily level, so about 6-8 months in, I took a break from it and established a more solid SOP and meditation practice. I then returned to daily Ogham, hoping I'd be able to find my daily readings pertinent (with using meditation to work on understanding correspondences). I've been back at it since August and for the most part there's still next to nothing that 'pings' in any way -- except when I do an "extra" divination with a specific question that's more than the "what's on for today" type. In those cases, Ogham is clear and to the point and I sense the resonances.

Last week I asked "Shall I continue with Ogham divination (today and through the end of the week <implying that I'll ask again about an upcoming period of time>)? I got Muin, Beith reversed, and Ngetal reversed. I took that to mean that while I'm attempting to connect with inspiration and prophecy, I'm wasting my effort longing for what isn't happening and that continuing (in spite of not being ready to really begin yet) is bound to result in more "stuck-in-a-rut-ness." Pretty clear to me!

The next day I asked, "Shall I continue to meditate on the fews and their elemental attributes? Ruis, Saille, Oir reversed, so - this way leads toward overcoming the difficulties, a transition to a new way of approaching Ogham, and that there's something that needs to happen on an unconscious level (intuition and dreaming) to release fixed ideas. I have to be patient and wait for outside forces to act. I took that, then, as a yes, just do the work and bide my time.

My question relates to my experience meditating on the elemental attributes. I'm getting few-related meanings that are different from the ones I have available to me - deeply layered and complex. but really quite different. Above, you answered Hana with, "that's why people who have completely different interpretations of the symbols of the oracle can both get accurate divinations with it." I can sense great energy and depth in what my meditations are hinting at (which is exciting), but I also recall your comment about Mr. Hughes's workings and the tendency of many to make a symbol mean what they want it to mean even though the symbol has a very clearly present egregor or meaning already.

Am I deluding myself (as a wet-behind-the-ears occultist) if I meditate my way to heretofore unrecognized Ogham meanings? Or is this possibly a way for me to meet Ogham at a mutual point of understanding? I don't want to go off half-cocked, but the meditation-inspired reflections have the potential to be significantly more alive for me than the lens through which I've been attempting to view each day's divination. But, you know... the magical resistance folks are also probably excited about their new and improved meanings for symbols too... ?

Thanks for your advice in advance!

replies.title: Re: rocky Ogham relationship
replies.content:
You're not deluding yourself. Divination in particular is all about working out the personal meanings of an oracle; it's like what happens in any other kind of relationship, where words come to have subtle nuances of meaning (or sometimes completely different meanings) closed to those who aren't part of the relationship. The proof of the pudding is in the eating; if you get good results, what you're doing works.

Hughes et al. are a good test case in this context. If their magic was working, then that would be evidence that the meanings they assigned to symbols were valid and appropriate. It's the fact that their magic has failed so consistently and spectacularly -- that their spell to keep Brett Kavanaugh out of the Supreme Court was promptly followed by the collapse of opposition to his appointment and his installation in that office, for example -- that shows that what they're doing is magically invalid.

About 3 years ago, a friend asked my partner and I if we would be willing to sign a petition to help her get registered as a wedding officiant in our province, to which we said yes. comments.title: Heathen Ritual

I've been doing the heathen lbrp.

I started shortly after dropping Christianity for Heathenry; so I started the ritual very much hoping it would deepen my connection to the pantheon.(for those of you who are curious, God and I had a really nice goodbye chat about it)I've frequently talked with the gods during this ritual. Thank you to you and Sven for a ritual practice that connected me to my gods.

Charlotte
